personality_voice.book_baseline: The books construct James Potter through two distinct temporal layers. As a teenager (Snape's Worst Memory, Deathly Hallows flashbacks), he is arrogant, casually cruel, performative, and attention-seeking — he bullies Snape unprovoked, plays to an audience, rumples his hair to look cool, steals a Snitch, and is only stopped by Lily's intervention. He is also genuinely talented, brave, and loyal to his friends. As an adult (Lily's letter, Sirius's accounts, the Resurrection Stone scene), he is described as having 'deflated his head,' showing warmth, humor, and protectiveness — making colored smoke for baby Harry, getting frustrated being cooped up but trying not to show it, dying wandlessly trying to hold off Voldemort. His voice in the Pensieve is sharp, mocking, and performative ('All right, Snivellus?', 'Who wants to see me take off Snivelly's trousers?'). His posthumous voice via the Resurrection Stone is brief but tender and encouraging.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films give James almost no direct characterization. In Philosopher's Stone, he appears only as a silent reflection in the Mirror of Erised, smiling and nodding. In Order of the Phoenix, the Pensieve scene is drastically compressed: James disarms Snape and says 'Right. Who wants to see me take off Snivelly's trousers?' — preserving the bullying behavior but stripping the extended performative cruelty, the Snitch-playing, the hair-rumpling, the audience-playing, and the complex reaction to Lily. In Deathly Hallows Part 2, James appears via the Resurrection Stone and says 'You're nearly there, son' and 'Until the end.' The teenage arrogance and the adult warmth-under-pressure are both present in fragments, but the film never builds either into a coherent personality.
personality_voice.difference: The film preserves the surface markers (bullying Snape, tender Resurrection Stone moment) but removes the behavioral texture that makes James legible as a character: the performative showing-off for Wormtail, the hair-rumpling vanity, the complex reaction to Lily's rejection, the casual theft of the Snitch, the way he only stops for Sirius. The adult James — frustrated but trying to hide it, playful with baby Harry, writing to Sirius — is entirely absent. The film James is a collection of two or three gestures rather than a personality.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core traits are recognizable but substantially thinned. The teenage arrogance survives in one line; the adult warmth survives in two lines. The contradictions, the performative vanity, the specific social dynamics, and the emotional texture are all missing. This is a 14–17 range: recognizable but important traits, tone, and contradictions are reduced.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for both book and film material present in the corpus.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: James functions across multiple narrative roles: (1) as a posthumous moral reference point whose legacy Harry must interrogate and complicate (the Pensieve revelation destabilizes Harry's idealization); (2) as an active agent in his own death scene — he tries to hold off Voldemort wandlessly, buying Lily time; (3) as a structural presence in the Marauder backstory, where his choices (becoming an Animagus to help Lupin, insisting on Pettigrew as Secret-Keeper, saving Snape from the Shrieking Shack) directly drive plot; (4) as a guide figure in the Resurrection Stone scene. His agency in the books is substantial even in death: his decisions caused the betrayal, his sacrifice enabled Harry's survival, and his character forces Harry to grow up morally.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In the films, James is almost entirely passive and symbolic. He appears as a silent reflection (PS), a brief Pensieve bully (OotP), a name on a gravestone (DH1), and a two-line guide figure (DH2). His active role in the Marauder backstory — the Animagus transformation, the Secret-Keeper decision, saving Snape — is either cut entirely or transferred to Lupin's narration in PoA (which is itself cut from the film). His wandless attempt to hold off Voldemort is narrated in the books but not dramatized in the films; the film version of Voldemort's attack on Godric's Hollow (DH1) shows James only as a body falling.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Marauder backstory is largely absent from the films, removing James's most significant acts of agency. The Secret-Keeper decision, the Animagus transformation, and the Snape rescue are all cut. The Pensieve scene is compressed to a single bullying moment without the broader context of James's social power and choices. His role as a moral complication for Harry — forcing Harry to question his idealization — is present in OotP but severely truncated.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: James's narrative agency is substantially reduced. His key decisions and actions are either cut or transferred to narration that doesn't appear in the films. His role as a moral complication for Harry is present but compressed to the point where it cannot fully function. This is a 10–13 range: role and agency are clearly altered.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for what is absent based on the corpus showing no film scenes corresponding to major book agency moments.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: James's motivations are layered. As a teenager: desire for status, peer approval (especially Sirius's), and attraction to Lily — all visible in the Pensieve. As an adult: love for Lily and Harry, loyalty to friends (which leads to the fatal trust in Pettigrew), and a protective instinct that costs him his life. His internal conflict is implicit rather than explicit: he is arrogant but capable of growth; he trusts too completely; he is brave to the point of recklessness (dying wandlessly). Lily's letter shows a man frustrated by confinement but suppressing it for his family's sake.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films provide almost no access to James's motivations. The Pensieve scene shows the bullying impulse but not the attraction to Lily or the peer-approval dynamic. The Resurrection Stone scene shows paternal love and pride but without the context of who James was or what he sacrificed. The letter, the Godric's Hollow backstory, and the Secret-Keeper decision — all of which illuminate his motivations — are absent or reduced to set dressing.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The motivational complexity (arrogance coexisting with love, trust leading to betrayal, bravery shading into recklessness) is almost entirely absent from the films. What remains is a sketch: he bullied people, he loved his son.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are partially present but so underexplained that they cannot support the same characterization. The internal conflict between arrogance and love, between trust and vulnerability, is not visible. This is an 8–10 range.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Reasonably inferred from the corpus; the film corpus is too limited to directly evidence motivational complexity.

character_arc.book_baseline: James has a clear arc across the books: from arrogant, bullying teenager (Pensieve) to a man who 'deflated his head' (Sirius, Lupin), married Lily, became a father, and died protecting his family. The arc is confirmed by multiple sources: Sirius ('once James had deflated his head a bit'), Lupin ('he would have saved Pettigrew'), Dumbledore ('he would have saved Pettigrew too'). The arc is not shown in real time but is reconstructed through testimony and flashback. It is essential to understanding James as a character — without it, the Pensieve scene leaves him simply as a bully.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films show the teenage James (OotP Pensieve) and the posthumous James (DH2 Resurrection Stone) but do not connect them with any arc. The growth from arrogant teenager to loving father is never established in the films. Sirius's comment about James 'deflating his head' is absent. The film audience sees a bully and then a tender ghost, with no bridge.
character_arc.difference: The arc exists in the books as a reconstructed trajectory confirmed by multiple characters. In the films, the two endpoints are present but the arc itself — the change, the growth, the reasons Lily eventually loved him — is entirely missing. The film cannot convey the arc because the testimony that establishes it (Sirius, Lupin, Dumbledore) is cut.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is incomplete and missing its most important step: the evidence of change. Without it, the film James is incoherent — a bully who is also a loving father, with no explanation. This is an 8–10 range: arc is incomplete and missing important steps.
character_arc.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for the book arc through corpus testimony; directly evidenced for the film's failure to connect the arc.

key_relationships.book_baseline: James's key relationships are: (1) Lily — complex, evolving from her contempt to eventual love, central to his growth; (2) Sirius — best friend, 'like brothers,' the relationship that defines his social world and leads to the Secret-Keeper decision; (3) Lupin — deep loyalty, the Animagus transformation done for Lupin's sake; (4) Pettigrew — misplaced trust that causes his death; (5) Snape — mutual hatred rooted in jealousy and cruelty, with the complicating fact that James saved Snape's life. Each relationship reveals something essential about James.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The films preserve almost none of these dynamics. The Lily relationship is reduced to a silent mirror reflection and a gravestone. The Sirius friendship is mentioned in PoA but the film cuts the scenes that dramatize it. The Lupin relationship (Animagus transformation) is cut from the PoA film. The Pettigrew relationship and the fatal trust are referenced but not dramatized. The Snape relationship survives in the OotP Pensieve but without the saving-Snape's-life complication.
key_relationships.difference: The emotional dynamics of all five key relationships are either absent or reduced to references. The Lily relationship — the most important — is never shown developing. The Sirius friendship loses its depth. The Lupin loyalty is cut. The Pettigrew betrayal loses its emotional weight because the trust was never established.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Key relationship dynamics are substantially altered or absent. The loss changes how James is understood — without the Lily arc, his growth is invisible; without the Sirius/Lupin/Pettigrew dynamics, his fatal trust is unmotivated. This is a 5–6 range.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present and absent in the corpus.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: James's complexity in the books rests on several tensions: he is a bully who becomes a good man; he is brave but reckless; he is loyal but his loyalty kills him; he is arrogant but capable of love; he saves the life of the man who hates him. The Pensieve scene is specifically designed to complicate Harry's idealization, and the subsequent testimony from Sirius, Lupin, and Dumbledore restores a more complete picture. The letter from Lily adds domestic warmth and suppressed frustration. The Godric's Hollow death scene (from Voldemort's POV) shows him dying wandlessly, trying to protect his family.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the bullying moment and the tender ghost, but remove almost all the material that makes James complex: the saving of Snape, the Animagus transformation, the Secret-Keeper decision, the letter, the domestic scenes, the testimony from Sirius and Lupin about his growth. The OotP Pensieve scene is present but compressed, and crucially, the film does not include Harry's subsequent moral reckoning with what he saw — the scene that makes the Pensieve moment narratively meaningful.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The contradictions, the growth, the fatal loyalty, the domestic warmth, and Harry's moral reckoning are all absent. What remains is a simplified figure: bully-as-teenager, loving-ghost-as-adult. The material that made James rich — his flaws, his growth, his relationships, his specific acts of agency — is almost entirely lost.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Major flattening. James is reduced to two gestures without the connective tissue that makes him a complex character. The lost material is not incidental — it is the substance of his characterization. This is a 5–7 range.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for what is absent based on the corpus.

lost_or_transferred_material: The extended Pensieve bullying scene (Snitch-playing, hair-rumpling, audience-playing, reaction to Lily's rejection) is compressed to a single line in the film
James saving Snape from the Shrieking Shack — the key complication of their relationship — is cut entirely from the films
The Animagus transformation backstory (James and Sirius becoming Animagi to help Lupin) is cut from the PoA film
The Secret-Keeper decision and James's insistence on using Pettigrew are referenced in PoA but the film cuts the Shrieking Shack scene where this is fully dramatized
Lily's letter from Godric's Hollow — showing James's domestic warmth, suppressed frustration, and playfulness with baby Harry — is absent from the films
Sirius's and Lupin's testimony about James 'deflating his head' and growing into a good man is cut, removing the arc's connective tissue
Harry's moral reckoning after the Pensieve scene — the extended internal conflict about whether he wants to be like his father — is absent from the film
Dumbledore's statement that James would have saved Pettigrew is cut
The Godric's Hollow death scene from Voldemort's POV (DH1 book) showing James dying wandlessly is not dramatized in the film
The photograph of the original Order of the Phoenix and Harry's recognition of his father in it is absent from the films
James's role in creating the Marauder's Map is referenced but not dramatized
The two-way mirror backstory (James and Sirius using them in detention) is cut
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if core book functions or acts of agency are transferred or removed): applied — James's key acts of agency (Animagus transformation, Secret-Keeper decision, saving Snape) are cut from the films

Cap 4 (score above 70 if films preserve only broad identity but remove most defining characterization): applied — the films preserve James's surface identity (bully-teenager, loving-ghost) but remove the material that defines his book characterization
Cap 1 (score above 85 if films remove major complexity, agency, contradiction, or key relationships): applied — all of the above
comparative.key_observations: James Potter is one of the most significantly reduced characters in the film adaptations relative to his book importance. The films preserve two data points — teenage bully and loving posthumous father — but remove almost everything that connects and complicates them: the growth arc, the key relationships, the fatal loyalty, the domestic warmth, and Harry's moral reckoning with his father's flaws. The result is a character who is recognizable in outline but hollow in substance. The OotP Pensieve scene is the films' most substantive engagement with James, but even there the compression removes the behavioral texture and the crucial aftermath. The Resurrection Stone scene is faithful in tone but too brief to carry characterization. The score of 56 reflects a portrayal that is superficially present but substantially incomplete.
